aliases: Cooked Tomato Salsa with Corn Chips

english: Cooked Tomato Salsa with Corn Chips

food_subgroup: Dips and chips / tortilla chips with salsa

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general salsa class (FOODON_03302050). No specific 'salsa roja with tortilla chips' combination class exists; closest_parent used for the salsa component. Tortilla chips are a separate food item not captured in this single code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers sauces and similar products. No specific salsa roja with chips composite code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent applied to the salsa component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 represents salsa as a substance. The combined dish (salsa roja with tortilla chips) has no dedicated SNOMED CT concept; closest_parent applied.

preparation_method: cooked (salsa: simmered/blended tomatoes, chillies, onion, garlic)
fried or baked (tortilla chips: corn masa fried or baked)
main_ingredients: corn tortilla chips (masa harina, vegetable oil, salt)
allergens: gluten (corn chips may be processed in shared facilities; traditional corn chips are gluten-free but cross-contamination risk exists)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack serving consists of approximately 150 g total, comprising roughly 50 g of salsa roja (about 3–4 tablespoons) and 100 g of tortilla chips (approximately 20–25 chips).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate: Salsa, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 170072) and Snacks, tortilla chips, plain (FDC ID 168535)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for 'salsa roja with tortilla chips' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for (1) salsa, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 170072) and (2) snacks, tortilla chips, plain (FDC ID 168535), weighted at approximately 33% salsa and 67% tortilla chips by mass to reflect a typical snack serving ratio. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — Corn chips / tortilla chips GI reference; salsa component GI estimated as low (tomato-based, low carbohydrate).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tortilla chips (corn chips) have a published GI of approximately 63 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Salsa roja is a tomato-based condiment with negligible glycemic contribution. The composite GI of this snack is dominated by the tortilla chips component and is estimated at 63 (medium). Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (63 × 69.75) / 100 ≈ 44.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g; ~585 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, as a 150 g serving provides approximately 585 mg sodium — roughly 25% of the recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — and should choose low-sodium tortilla chip varieties where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 63) driven by tortilla chips; moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~70 g carbohydrate per 150 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the medium GI and high carbohydrate content of tortilla chips can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; pairing with the tomato-based salsa roja (which has a low GI and provides fibre) may modestly attenuate the glycemic response, but total carbohydrate intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (~16.8 g per 100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g); elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the relatively low saturated fat profile of this snack is favourable, but the elevated sodium content and energy density of tortilla chips warrant portion control; the salsa roja component contributes beneficial lycopene and vitamin C from tomatoes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salsa Roja with Tortilla Chips is a classic Mexican snack consisting of two components: a cooked red salsa (salsa roja) and crispy corn tortilla chips (totopos). Salsa roja is prepared by simmering or dry-roasting tomatoes, dried red chillies (such as guajillo or ancho), onion, and garlic, then blending the mixture into a smooth or slightly chunky sauce seasoned with salt. The result is a deeply flavoured, mildly spicy, tangy condiment with a rich red-orange colour. Tortilla chips are made from corn masa (nixtamalised maize dough) that is cut into triangles and either fried or baked until crisp and golden, then salted. Together, the combination offers a contrast of textures — the crunchy, salty chips against the smooth, zesty salsa — and is widely consumed as a snack, appetiser, or accompaniment to Mexican meals. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense due to the tortilla chips, which contribute the majority of carbohydrates, fat, and calories. The salsa roja component is low in calories but provides lycopene (from tomatoes), vitamin C, and dietary fibre. The composite dish has a medium glycemic index (approximately 63), driven by the corn chips, and a high glycemic load per standard serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high, primarily from the salted chips and seasoned salsa, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free (when made with 100% corn chips), vegan, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a range of dietary preferences. In Singapore, this dish is served at Mexican restaurants such as Three Amigos and Fiesta Mexico, typically as a starter or snack at a price point of approximately SGD 11.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salsa and chips?
Is salsa roja with chips gluten-free?
What is the GI of tortilla chips?
Can diabetics eat tortilla chips with salsa?
Sodium content of salsa and chips
Mexican snack with red sauce and chips

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for 'salsa roja with tortilla chips' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for salsa (FDC ID 170072) and plain tortilla chips (FDC ID 168535) at an approximate 33:67 mass ratio. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn/tortilla chips (GI ~63). Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimate from approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate from two USDA SR Legacy entries (salsa, ready-to-serve FDC 170072 and tortilla chips, plain FDC 168535) weighted at approximately 33% salsa and 67% chips by mass. Direct measurement of this specific restaurant dish has not been performed. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both component foods; tortilla chips made with vegetable oil contain negligible industrially produced trans fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; both salsa and corn tortilla chips are plant-based foods containing no cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both component foods; plant-based snack foods do not naturally contain vitamin D. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (7.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (5.8), potassium_mg (230.0), calcium_mg (52.0), iron_mg (1.4), vitamin_c_mg (4.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.05), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.1), vitamin_b6_mg (0.12), folate_mcg_dfe (22.0), vitamin_e_mg (1.3), phosphorus_mg (110.0), magnesium_mg (38.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (4.5), water_g (28.5) are all estimated from the USDA composite and should be treated as approximate. 7. GI of 63 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for corn chips; the salsa roja component has negligible glycemic contribution. GL per 150 g serving = (63 × 69.75) / 100 ≈ 44.0. 8. This dish is served at Mexican restaurants in Singapore (field survey: Fiesta Mexico — Three Amigos, Three Amigos Mexican and Indian — Boat Quay) at SGD 11.00. 9. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this Mexican dish beyond the Spanish romanized form 'Salsa Roja con Totopos'; no Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi names are in common use for this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from 'SALSA ROJAS WITH CHIPS' to canonical 'Salsa Roja with Tortilla Chips'.
Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for salsa (FDC 170072) and tortilla chips (FDC 168535) at 33:67 mass ratio.
Documented all zero-value nutrients (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with confirmed-zero justifications in notes.
Assigned GI of 63 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for corn/tortilla chips as analogous estimate.
Selected defensible ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) with closest_parent match type and gap notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance: Mexican cuisine has no mandatory non-Latin script requirement; romanized Spanish alias provided.
